tweets.text: The war aside, the basic and most fundamental problem Iran is facing is that it has a revolutionary state but with a disenchanted population, when the gap between regime and political authority widens, the regime loses gradually its coercive capacity. This is something IRGC doesn’t have an answer to.

This is something IRGC doesn’t have an answer to.

For those who are interested in Sahel and North Africa, just sharing some books I found interesting, useful, and helpful:

- Mansa Musa and the Empire of Mali by P. James Oliver  
- The Islamic State in Africa: The Emergence, Evolution, and Future of the Next Jihadist Battlefront

tweets.text:
not really one to be too hyperbolic and sell insane dreams promising 100x, or 1000x here and there. typically i say it like it is

the reality of crypto is this. everything in crypto over a long enough time frame goes to 0. if you understand that. thats a big first step. that is a fact 99.99% of the time. its a giant game of musical chairs. some people are better at noticing when there are fewer and fewer chairs and the music is about to stop than others. this could be in terms of macro, or attention/themes

there are people who take winnings out in the bull market. and then there are the people who keep their winnings in, and those winnings get taken out by someone else. if you dont take it out, it doesnt belong to you, and it's always going to end up in the hands of someone more skilled. 

most things you trade should just be viewed as short term rentals. most people with a voice will tell you what you want to hear bc they are incentivized to do so in countless ways

you have to be cold blooded in this market

tweets.text:
My personal ranking of the best crypto cards available in US ↓

https://t.co/AM8jxsVJAW has tiers based on card adoption metrics which is already useful; I tried to rank by usability.

Tier S: @Plasma, @ether_fi
I spend most with these two + have some $$ deposited to earn APY.
Plasma earns cashback in XPL, EtherFi – in ETH.

Tier A: @KASTxyz, @xplaceapp, @coinbase card
XPlace is imo a very undervalued crypto card, under the radar play

Tier B: @lava_xyz, @hyperbeat, @Cypher_HQ_
All three are cool, each with some unique features (like BTC yield in the case of Lava)

Tier C: @itstuyo, @useTria
Tuyo is an emerging one with an interesting concept; might go higher.
Tria has some community sceptisism recently, so I can't put it higher – but still on the list.

What's your ranking?

tweets.text:
saylor is extremely good at one thing: raising copious amounts of capital. i do not have advice on that front

but when it comes to the inner technical workings of bitcoin he proved many times that he has no idea what he’s talking about

a few years ago he proudly announced on stage that his company will revolutionize the workplace by releasing software that will incentivize workers to show up on time to zoom calls by sending them fractions of a cent over lightning every time they do

truly a uniquely retarded idea that never launched, but also technically infeasible

saylor would go on every podcast for years announcing that ethereum isn’t necessary because we have lightning. a low iq comment that only someone doing no research whatsoever could ever say, and he didn’t say it once he said it seventy thousand times

of course when he actually needed a blockchain for his own purposes, to popularize STRC among crypto natives, he didn’t use lightning. he chose to promote a protocol that wraps STRC on ethereum

in another company keynote he proudly announced that microstrategy is developing ground-breaking software to store corporate documents in inscriptions on the bitcoin chain

not only was it politically tone-deaf (every bitcoiner hated inscriptions except for some wizard doodlers) and practically idiotic (there is no reason whatsoever to put corporate documents on a chain) it was also technology impossible. the chain is small, on purpose, and it cannot contain documents for every corporation.

when the mining industry was in flux during the mining exodus from china, saylor tried to stabilize things by starting a “miner council” he’d lead. on the first call of said council, mining leaders from across the globe listened to a multi-hour lecture where he rambled about how bitcoin is better than litecoin because it has more hashes per second (not knowing that litecoin’s hashing algorithm is entirely different from bitcoin’s hence the hash rates cannot be compared)

they couldn’t believe the confident boomer who invited them all to observe his complete and utter lack of knowledge in the field. they thought that because he’s rich he’d offer financial support, but he did not. he offered parables and monologues. the council never convened again

when asked about quantum resistance on major mainstream platforms, saylor routinely offers long monologues rehashing 15-year old forum posts on bitcointalk, not knowing the essence of what he says or why it no longer applies 15 years later, further convincing viewers that bitcoiners are lost

one of the greatest risks to bitcoin is allowing a single man to become its only spokesperson and decision maker. it’s especially risky if that man’s unprecedented access to capital allows him to be wrong for an exceptionally long time without changing his mind, while making him increasingly confident that he’s right.

tweets.text:
𝗧𝗵𝗲 $ZIM 𝗕𝘂𝘆𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝗜𝘀 𝗨𝗻𝗼𝗳𝗳𝗶𝗰𝗶𝗮𝗹𝗹𝘆 𝗢𝘃𝗲𝗿

The $ZIM buyout is not happening. It was just announced in the Israeli media that Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu opposes it. For me, this was over a long time ago.

What amazed me was watching people on X, who know absolutely nothing about Israeli politics, gamble on an Israeli M&A as if they had any edge. If you were one of them, look in the mirror. Hopefully you’ll learn something from this and not repeat the same mistake.

I said it many times: unless you understand Israeli politics and the political constraints around a deal like this, you have no edge. Your opinion on whether this deal closes is just noise.

I recommended buying $ZIM around $13.5. I recommended selling above $29. Looking back, this was probably one of the easiest investments I’ve ever made.

At the beginning, dumb analysts that heard about a place called Israel only in movies, were giving a company with roughly $22-24 per share in cash, plus an operating shipping business, price targets of $6. That told me they were blindly applying shipping multiples while ignoring the balance sheet.

Then verified reports of buyout interest started coming out of Israel, yet some Norwegian shipping hedge funds stayed short because they were focused only on the shipping cycle. They completely missed that the story had shifted from a shipping thesis to an M&A thesis.

Later, when the stock approached $30, I recommended selling because I believed the M&A optionality was unlikely to materialize as reports from Israeli media indicated the Israeli government opposed the transaction.

At that point, the mistake shifted from institutional investors to retail investors. Many convinced themselves the deal would happen despite having no understanding of Israeli politics or the government’s role.

I’ll publish a full update on $ZIM soon. For now, based on the latest reports, the buyout thesis appears to be effectively dead.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
$ZIM Buyout Math
(I have never seen something like this)
•Shares outstanding: 120M
•Current share price: $13.6
•Buyout offer: $22/share
•Total cost of buyout:
22 × 120M = $2.6B

Cash & Assets
•Cash per share: $24
•Total cash: 24 × 120M = $2.88B
•Additional assets: –$5B https://t.co/8DziQbCOcX

tweets.text: The problem with truth is, once you see it, you can’t unsee it.
tweets.full_text: The problem with truth is, once you see it, you can’t unsee it.

tweets.text:
13 days.

So far no one has found it.

My only hint is that I would encourage people to somewhat broaden their search; I've seen quite a few searches and AI scripts that fail to include categories of documents that really should be included.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
There have recently been claims that AI text analysis will make online anonymity untenable.

So let me cannibalize a piece of my own anonymity to do an experiment.

At some point this decade, I wrote a published document of medium importance to Ethereum - I estimate ~200 to 2000
tweets.full_text:
13 days.

So far no one has found it.

My only hint is that I would encourage people to somewhat broaden their search; I've seen quite a few searches and AI scripts that fail to include categories of documents that really should be included.

80 percent of Iranians don’t support the regime, they were protesting in huge numbers, America and Israel attacked the regime relentlessly killing its leader and many of the top officials, and yet here we are, they are more dug in and defiant. 

Lesson?   My lesson is  ending dictatorial regimes is not easy. Fear paralyses.   And under-estimating their cunning is a big mistake.  They took the strongest nation on earth to a draw and we gave up. 

The US will be fine. Pride is hurt for not accomplishing our goals in a war that cost lives and $$.   It will hurt Republicans in the midterms, etc  

The real losers here are the Iranian people who remain under the boot of a theocratic and autocratic group of ass hats.

tweets.text:
Wrt the token<>equity debate, theoretically even Hyperliquid is not perfect in terms of structure, because Jeff / the team are still free to "soft rug" the tokenholders and stop using 99% of the profit to buy back $HYPE, BUT so far they have proven that they were worthy of trust, and they have been treating the token as if it was equity.

This sets the bar for what buyers now expect from the s-tier teams that launch tokens.
tweets.full_text:

tweets.text: @Fiskantes Absolutely
tweets.full_text: @Fiskantes Absolutely

tweets.text:
All of this is irrelevant because it doesn't address my main point. If it's complicated to raise via a token, and you know you'll do it via equity, then don't launch the token in the first place? Or be *extremely* clear from the beginning that it's a dual structure and that the token is purely a utility token that does XYZ and nothing more.

But no, teams want the double dip of benefitting from the liquid public crypto markets and their upside, on top of having equity.
